Closed Cycle Plasma Amplifier
Dynamic Plasma Alternator

System Overview System Architecture Subsystem Deep Dive Operating Cycle — 10 Steps Build Instructions — 9 Phases Bill of Materials Theoretical Basis & Physics Safety Protocols Disclaimers & Research Notes

System Overview

This device is a hybrid closed-cycle energy system combining principles from magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) power generation, adiabatic plasma compression, and microwave-assisted ionization. In a conventional MHD generator, a high-velocity ionized gas passes through a magnetic field and an electric current is extracted via electrodes. This design adapts that concept into a sealed, recirculating system where ion-seeded gas (mercury + SF6) is cyclically ionized, compressed, and used to generate electrical output through a plasma alternator principle — with the ions recycled rather than exhausted.

The system is designated the Closed Cycle Plasma Amplifier and Dynamic Plasma Alternator. The working principle is analogous to a conventional alternator's field excitation (B+), but instead of a spinning rotor, a magnetized plasma column oscillating at 10–30 Hz induces current in surrounding coils.

System Architecture

The system comprises 12 interconnected subsystems organized into four functional groups:

GroupSubsystemsPurpose
Energy InputsHVHF Ionizer, Magnetron, Linear Servo Piston, Filament HeaterProvide ionization energy, microwave excitation, mechanical compression
Plasma CoreIon Seed Gas, Hastelloy Screens, Magnetic Coils, Main ChamberContain and sustain the ionized plasma
Energy ExtractionDynamic Plasma Alternator, Output CollimatorConvert plasma energy to usable electrical output
Thermal ManagementWater Jacket, Ion RecirculationManage heat and recycle working fluid

Subsystem Deep Dive

① HVHF Ionizer — 20kV AC @ 300kHz

The high-voltage, high-frequency field excites and ionizes the seed gas within the chamber, creating a conductive plasma. This functions identically to the field excitation winding on a conventional alternator. The 300 kHz frequency allows efficient coupling into the ion seed gas without significant dielectric losses.

  • Topology: Resonant half-bridge inverter with HV flyback transformer
  • Turns ratio: ~1:400 for 48V→20kV
  • Core: N87 ferrite, designed for 300kHz
  • Output: ceramic HV feedthrough into chamber
  • Safety: 10MΩ bleed resistors, HV interlock on all access panels

② Ion Seed Gas — Mercury (Hg) + SF6

Mercury vapor provides easily ionizable atoms (ionization energy: 10.44 eV). SF6 has an extremely high electron affinity — neutral SF6 molecules capture free electrons to form stable negative ions (SF6⁻), serving as a buffer gas and electron-capture moderator that controls ionization dynamics and prevents uncontrolled arc discharge.

  • Replenishment: small dose every 10–20 hours
  • Control: solenoid vent + auto-dosing timer
  • Wire feed: continuous metal ion replenishment via filament zone
⚠️ Mercury is a toxic heavy metal. SF6 is an asphyxiant and potent greenhouse gas (GWP ~23,500×CO₂). Full PPE, sealed system, and regulatory compliance are mandatory.

③ Hastelloy X Screens

Hastelloy X is a nickel-base superalloy (Ni 47.5%, Cr 21.8%, Fe 18.5%, Mo 9%) with oxidation resistance to 2200°F (1204°C), maintaining ductility after 16,000+ hours at 1200–1600°F.

  • Role: plasma containment barriers, heat shields, ion filtering elements
  • Specification: 20–40 mesh woven cloth, 3" wide
  • Assembly: 2-layer removable cartridge with 0.25" spacer rings

④ Magnetron — 1GHz+ / 80W

Provides supplemental microwave energy to sustain plasma between piston compression strokes. At atmospheric pressure, 80W at 1GHz can maintain plasma temperatures above ignition threshold.

  • Coupling: waveguide with boron nitride or alumina pressure window
  • Protection: circulator + dummy load for reflected power
  • Power supply: 4kV DC @ ~100mA regulated

⑤ Linear Servo Piston — 3000W

The mechanical heart of the system. From the original schematic: "Piston has full command of adiabatic pressure. At times it will barely moving in stroke, expanding and compressing volume in place. Pump action is almost no movement till ignition temperature is reached. Then long slow 10–30Hz oscillations take place."

  • Drive: 3000W permanent magnet linear motor
  • Piston: Inconel 718 or chrome-moly, 1500°F rated
  • Cylinder: 4130/4140 Chrome-Moly steel
  • Control: Profile A (startup): ±0.5mm @ 0.5–2Hz | Profile B (running): ±15–50mm @ 10–30Hz

⑥ Magnetic Confinement Coils

Solenoid coils wound around the main chamber exterior confine the plasma radially, shape the plasma column, and generate the transverse magnetic field through which ionized gas moves — the fundamental MHD interaction principle.

  • Wire: Class H magnet wire, 14 AWG (or ceramic-insulated for hot zones)
  • Target: 0.1–0.5 Tesla at plasma center
  • Turns: 50–100 per section × 4–6 sections

⑦ Water Jacket

Helical copper or SS tubing around cylinder exterior. The plasma core exceeds 1500°F while chrome-moly cylinder must stay below ~1100°F to maintain structural integrity.

  • Flow rate: minimum 2 GPM for 3kW heat rejection
  • Coolant: distilled water + 10% propylene glycol
  • Sensors: 6× K-type thermocouples, flow meter

⑧ Output Collimator

Focuses and directs the extracted energy output. Contains magnetic lens elements and graduated aperture plates aligned with the plasma column axis.

  • Housing: 316L non-magnetic stainless steel
  • Lenses: 2 electromagnetic coil sets (variable focus)
  • Pickup: induction coils (14 AWG, 50 turns) + tungsten-rhenium electrode probes

⑨ Dynamic Plasma Alternator

The 10–30Hz piston oscillations create a pulsating magnetized plasma column. This oscillating conductive medium, within the static confinement field, induces an alternating EMF in surrounding coils — the Faraday principle applied to plasma instead of a copper rotor.

  • Output frequency: directly controlled by servo oscillation rate (10–30Hz)
  • Output voltage: function of plasma conductivity × field strength × oscillation amplitude
  • Conditioning: rectifier/inverter module for usable DC or AC output

Operating Cycle — 10 Steps

  1. Ion Seeding — Mercury and SF6 mixture introduced through solenoid vent. Partial pressures controlled to optimize ionization balance.
  2. Filament Heating — Tungsten filament heater activates. Thermionic emission releases initial electrons and metal ion vapor.
  3. HVHF Excitation — 20kV @ 300kHz ionizer field applied. Electrons stripped from mercury atoms; weakly ionized plasma forms.
  4. Magnetron Boost — 1GHz+ magnetron injects 80W microwave energy. Plasma temperature increases through dielectric absorption.
  5. Adiabatic Micro-Compression — Servo piston makes micro-displacements (±0.5mm). Temperature rises adiabatically with almost no visible travel.
  6. Ignition Temperature — Compression heating raises plasma to self-sustaining temperature (~2000–3000K). Ionization becomes self-maintaining.
  7. Sustained Oscillation — Piston transitions to 10–30Hz full oscillation cycles. Rhythmic compression and expansion creates pulsating plasma column.
  8. MHD Power Extraction — Oscillating plasma column within confinement field induces AC current in alternator coils.
  9. Collimator Output — Output collimator focuses and directs extracted energy beam.
  10. Ion Recirculation — Closed cycle returns working gas to input. Ions replenished every 10–20 hours via solenoid dosing.

Build Instructions — 9 Phases

Phase 1 — Main Cylinder (Frame)

Materials: 4130/4140 chrome-moly steel tube, 3" bore × 36" length, 0.375" wall minimum

  1. Machine cylinder bore to ±0.001" tolerance; hone interior for piston seal
  2. Machine end cap flanges with O-ring grooves (Viton or Kalrez seals)
  3. Drill/tap ports: magnetron waveguide, gas inlet, solenoid vent, pressure transducer × 1, thermocouple × 4
  4. Stress-relieve at 1150°F, 1 hour per inch of wall thickness
  5. Inspect with dye penetrant or ultrasonic testing

Phase 2 — Linear Servo Piston

Materials: 3000W PM linear motor with servo driver, Inconel 718 piston head, ceramic-coated piston rings

  1. Mount linear motor to end flange via precision adapter plate
  2. Machine piston head to bore with 0.001" clearance
  3. Install 2 compression rings + 1 scraper ring
  4. Connect servo driver with optical linear encoder
  5. Program motion profiles: A (±0.5mm @ 0.5–2Hz startup) and B (±15–50mm @ 10–30Hz running)

Phase 3 — Magnetic Confinement Coils

Materials: Class H magnet wire 14 AWG, iron/ferrite core segments, PTFE or ceramic bobbins

  1. Wind solenoid coils around chamber exterior, 4–6 sections along length
  2. 50–100 turns per section; layer-wind and pot with high-temp epoxy
  3. Wire series (high-voltage driver) or parallel (high-current driver)
  4. Target field: 0.1–0.5 Tesla at plasma center
  5. Connect to adjustable DC power supply with current metering

Phase 4 — Hastelloy Screen Insert

Materials: Hastelloy X mesh 20 mesh, machined Hastelloy X mounting rings

  1. Cut screens to bore diameter with 0.010" clearance
  2. Assemble 2-layer cartridge with 0.25" spacer rings
  3. Make cartridge removable for inspection and replacement
  4. Verify ≥80% free-area flow path
  5. Install deformation sensor to detect screen failure

Phase 5 — Water Jacket (Cooling)

Materials: 3/8" OD copper tubing 20 ft, circulation pump 1–5 GPM, plate heat exchanger

  1. Wrap tubing in tight helical pattern around cylinder; braze to surface
  2. Connect inlet/outlet to pump and heat exchanger loop
  3. Install T-type thermocouples: inlet, outlet, mid-cylinder
  4. Pressure test cooling loop to 100 PSI
  5. Fill with distilled water + 10% propylene glycol

Phase 6 — Electronics (Ionizer + Magnetron)

HVHF Ionizer:

  1. Build resonant half-bridge inverter: 48V DC → 20kV AC @ 300kHz
  2. N87 ferrite transformer, turns ratio ~1:400
  3. Output filter: 1nF / 30kV ceramic capacitor
  4. HV interlock on all access panels; 10MΩ bleed resistors across output
  5. Feed through alumina 30kV-rated HV feedthrough into chamber

Magnetron:

  1. Source 1GHz cavity magnetron; build waveguide coupling to chamber port
  2. Install boron nitride pressure window (microwave transparent, 1500°F rated)
  3. Add circulator + dummy load for reflected power protection
  4. Magnetron power supply: 4kV DC @ 100mA regulated
  5. Interlock to plasma temperature sensor — don't fire magnetron cold

Phase 7 — Ion Seed Gas System ⚠️ HAZMAT

Mercury is toxic. SF6 is an asphyxiant and GHG. Full PPE, ventilation, sealed system, and environmental compliance are required at all times.
  1. Build sealed 316L SS reservoir for Hg/SF6 mixture
  2. Install SS bellows-sealed solenoid valve for controlled injection
  3. Connect tungsten filament heater at gas inlet (5–10A supply)
  4. Install motorized wire feed spool for continuous metal ion replenishment
  5. Pressure test gas system to 2× operating pressure with argon
  6. Initial charge: 10–50mL mercury + SF6 at 0.1–1 atm partial pressure
  7. Set auto-dosing timer relay for 10–20 hour replenishment cycles
  8. Install mercury vapor detector (alarm at 0.025 mg/m³)

Phase 8 — Output Collimator Assembly

  1. Machine collimator housing from 316L non-magnetic SS
  2. Install 3 sets of gradient-aperture plates (graduated hole sizes)
  3. Wind 2 electromagnetic lens coils around housing (variable DC supply)
  4. Mount induction pickup coils: 14 AWG, 50 turns each
  5. Install tungsten-rhenium electrode probes for direct plasma current tap
  6. Route output leads to rectifier / conditioning module

Phase 9 — Testing & Commissioning

  1. Leak Test — Argon to 1.5× operating pressure; verify all seals
  2. Cooling Test — Run water jacket 30 min; verify flow/temp delta
  3. Servo Test — Run piston through all motion profiles; verify position accuracy
  4. HV Test — Power ionizer with inert gas; verify 20kV on HV probe
  5. Magnetron Test — Test magnetron isolated; verify power output
  6. Cold Gas Test — Introduce argon; run piston compression; verify pressure traces
  7. First Plasma — Small Hg/SF6 charge + all systems → look for blue-white plasma glow through sight glass
  8. Oscillation Ramp — Increase piston frequency to 10–30Hz; monitor pressure, temp, and induced coil voltage
  9. Power Measurement — Measure RMS voltage/current on output coils vs. total input power
  10. Thermal Soak — Run 2+ hours; verify water jacket maintains safe temperatures throughout

Theoretical Basis & Physics

The underlying physics combines several well-established principles from plasma physics and electromagnetism:

MHD Principle

The fundamental equation governing MHD power extraction is the Lorentz force law applied to a conducting fluid:

F = q(E + v × B)

When ionized gas moves through a transverse magnetic field B at velocity v, an EMF is induced: EMF = v × B × L where L = electrode spacing. For plasma with electrical conductivity σ, the extracted current density: J = σ(E + v × B)

Adiabatic Compression

For an ideal gas compressed adiabatically (no heat exchange): T₂/T₁ = (V₁/V₂)^(γ-1) where γ = ratio of specific heats (≈1.67 for monatomic plasma). A 10:1 compression ratio yields a temperature ratio of ~3.98×. Starting at 500K, this produces ~2000K — sufficient for mercury plasma ignition.

Saha Ionization Equation

Degree of ionization follows the Saha equation. For mercury (ionization energy = 10.44 eV), significant ionization occurs above ~3000K at atmospheric pressure. SF6 seeding modifies this by electron capture, stabilizing the plasma against runaway discharge.

Plasma Alternator Output

Peak induced voltage in pickup coils: V_peak = N × B × A × ω × sin(ωt) where N = coil turns, B = field strength, A = plasma cross-section area, ω = 2πf (10–30Hz).

Historical MHD Context

NASA Lewis Research Center demonstrated closed-loop MHD generation using cesium-seeded argon plasma. The Soviet "Pamir" MHD program achieved MW-scale output at 2500–3000K. These systems required substantial external energy input — all verified MHD generators consume more energy than they produce, though advances in plasma confinement and seeding continue to improve efficiency ratios.

Safety Protocols

⚠️ THIS SYSTEM INVOLVES MULTIPLE SERIOUS HAZARDS. A FULL SAFETY PLAN, RISK ASSESSMENT, AND QUALIFIED SUPERVISION ARE REQUIRED BEFORE ANY BUILD OR OPERATION.
HazardLevelControls Required
Mercury Vapor ToxicityCRITICALFully sealed system; Hg vapor detector (alarm @ 0.025 mg/m³); full-face respirator + N100/OV cartridges; secondary containment tray; licensed hazmat disposal
SF6 AsphyxiationHIGHO₂ monitor in workspace; ventilation system; never work in low areas; buddy system protocol; SF6 recovery system (no venting to atmosphere)
High Voltage 20kV ACCRITICALHardware interlocks on all panels; 10MΩ bleed resistors; lockout/tagout procedure; one-hand rule; HV insulating gloves + face shield; rubber insulating mat
Microwave RadiationMODERATEShielded waveguide; RF-tight joints; leakage survey before operation; microwave-opaque enclosure; panel-open interlock kills magnetron
High Temperature 1500°F+HIGHWater jacket active whenever plasma running; thermal runaway alarm on all 6 thermocouples; auto-shutdown if flow drops; Hastelloy screen integrity check each run
High PressureMODERATEChrome-moly cylinder rated to 3× operating pressure; pressure relief valve at 1.2× operating; burst disc as secondary; no personnel within 10 ft during initial pressure tests

Emergency Procedure

  1. Hit emergency stop — kills servo + all HV simultaneously
  2. Actuate solenoid vent — safely vent gas system to recovery canister
  3. Allow 5 minutes cooldown before approaching machine
  4. Verify mercury vapor detector reading is below alarm threshold before removing any covers
  5. Document incident; do not restart without engineering review

Disclaimers & Research Notes

  1. Thermodynamic Disclaimer: The laws of thermodynamics dictate that no closed system produces more energy than is input. The "free energy" concept here refers to the aspiration of using plasma physics to minimize mechanical work input — not a claim of perpetual motion or over-unity operation. Experimental measurement of net energy balance is essential.
  2. Experimental Status: This is a prototype-level design. No verified performance data exists for this specific configuration. Professional engineering review, failure mode analysis (FMEA), and incremental testing are required.
  3. Regulatory Compliance: Mercury (EPA, OSHA PEL 0.1 mg/m³), SF6 (Kyoto Protocol, EPA Section 608), High Voltage (NEC, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.303), Pressure Vessels (ASME BPVC).
  4. Open Source License: This blueprint is released for open research use. No warranty of any kind is expressed or implied. The builder/innovator assumes all risk.
  5. Privacy: Personal names from the original hand-drawn schematic have been removed from this document to protect individual privacy. The design is preserved and documented faithfully.
